Similarly, text messages relating to the children not attending swimming lessons were inappropriate. The mother sent messages such as, “you need to go because I pay for this”. She told X she borrowed money from his grandfather to meet the costs. The mother made X responsible for his attendance at swimming, which he had no control over.

In other text exchanges the mother makes X feel responsible for his brother. An example is telling X he should go to bed early to be with Y, because Y needs his love, and he makes Y feel safe and calm.

On 18 May 2025, the night before interviews for the Update Report, the mother sent a series of texts to X. She gave evidence she was telling X to tell the truth. That is not a fair assessment of the messages sent. She listed matters he should raise with the report writer. When cross-examined she was furious at the father getting a dog two days before the interview. She thought this was sufficient justification for her directing X in the manner she did.

I accept the dog purchase two days before the interview was likely done by the father to promote his position to the boys. Of itself it did not cause harm to the children. This does not justify the mother’s conduct.

An order was made on 6 February 2025 restraining each of the parties from involving the children in the court proceedings and denigrating the other parent. I accept that the mother was not complying with that order.

The text messages with X suggest the mother can react in a compulsive way at times when messaging X. She continues to text after he has responded, on the same topic. The messages place expectations on him which are completely unreasonable. I accept receiving messages from his mother in these circumstances would have been overwhelming and preoccupying for X. The mother’s conduct put X in the middle of conflict between his parents, which she created. She has done this with no thought about the emotional impact on him.

These children have seen both their parents be arrested by the police. In neither case did the other parent attempt to buffer them from that experience.

When their mother was arrested in 2023 her brother was present. He described the children as very upset, yelling, crying and begging for her not to be taken. She was handcuffed and placed in a paddy wagon. Four officers were present. I accept this was a terrible experience for the children. I accept it was an overreaction to her conduct at that time.

When their father was arrested in December 2023 the children were in the car with their maternal uncle and mother. They were driving to a changeover. I accept the mother and her brother expected changeover would not happen, because the father would be arrested.

The mother telephoned the police on hands free in the car. I accept the boys could hear that conversation. The mother stopped near the location for changeover, waiting for the police to arrive. Their uncle left them in the car to observe and record the incident on his telephone.

The uncle’s evidence was contradictory. At one point he said the children “had no awareness of the situation”. I do not accept at any point that could have been true. They heard the telephone call between their mother and the police. Their mother and uncle discussed the situation during the drive.

I accept the mother and her brother exposed the children to their father’s arrest. I accept this would have been at least bewildering if not frightening for the children. Those people were acutely aware of the toll on the children of the mother’s arrest. They had no insight that they were deliberately exposing the children to a repeat of that situation.

It is concerning that the extended families have involved themselves in the dispute. In December 2024 the maternal uncle and maternal grandmother attended a Christmas carol event at the boys’ school. The mother was prevented from attending due to the AVO. The uncle followed the father and children after the event because his car was parked in the same street. He did not wait to let them go, or cross the street. He walked past the father and children, who were getting into their car. The father complained to the police that he was threatened. Footage was available from one of the houses nearby which demonstrated there was no altercation. The police did not take the matter further.

The significance of this occasion is that the boys were again in the middle of conflict between people they love. The mother’s brother had the opportunity to wait and not put the children in that situation.

The mother’s brother has sent text messages to the father such as, “keep up the bullshit charade for someone who doesn’t know you”. He denied the messages were threatening. His evidence is they were “straight to the point”. I accept the texts were unnecessary and could be broadly described as threatening.

The report writer noted that X had an increased level of distress during the interviews for the second report. Y is reported by the mother to have heightened anxiety and panic attacks. The report writer did not allocate responsibility to either parent for the children’s distress, but was concerned at the children’s exposure to their conflict.

The children have spent more time with their mother over the course of their lives. She is the parent connected to the school, their friends’ parents, who makes arrangements for them. Ms ZZ’s affidavit supports the mother’s evidence that during the relationship the father left organising the children to the mother. That is, I accept, the lived experience of these children. It is not a criticism of the father but an observation about how these children have experienced being cared for by their parents. This may well give a greater level of comfort to the children in living in their mother’s home, than their father’s.

The mother’s psychologist has referred to her as having a severe level of anxiety, and depressed mood. The psychologist recommended she have ongoing treatment with a practitioner with experience in family law matters. His last session with the mother was 19 August 2024. There is no evidence she has consulted a psychologist since. The report writer posited six appointments with the psychiatrist Dr AA over almost two years does not suggest therapeutic work, but check ins for medication.

The mother’s anxious behaviour, such as texting X is less likely to be contained by orders that the children spend more time with the father. The evidence does not support that she has engaged in this kind of conduct when the children are with her. Her care for the children prior to separation does not suggest similar difficulties. Rather, the financial issues discovered at separation, the children living primarily with the father after October 2023, and the father remaining in the former matrimonial home all appear to have caused her difficulties in managing her behaviour and emotional response.

I accept that proceedings being finalised will assist the mother’s behaviour. Unfortunately, the property proceedings remain outstanding. The mother should engage with a therapist to manage her anxiety, particularly while litigation remains on foot.

VIEWS

The children are not of an age or stage of maturity when I would consider their views to be determinative, although they should be taken into account.

The children have expressed a desire to live primarily with their mother across the interviews for both reports. The children have presented as close to both parents, although they desire more time with their mother.

I accept that the children missed their mother when they were no longer living with her. This may be a function of her being their primary carer throughout their lives. Despite the forceful submissions made on the father’s part about the mother’s conduct, I note the orders he seeks are for substantial time with her. I accept this is an acknowledgment of the close relationship she and the children share.

At the second interviews X expressed suspicion of his father, such as getting a dog as a bribe. I accept the mother expressed this in texts to X prior to the interview. This was not a conclusion X reached. The report writer was concerned the children’s views at the second report were less candid than for the first report. I take this as indicative of the toll of the conflict on the children.

WHAT LIVE WITH AND SPEND TIME WITH ARRANGEMENTS ARE IN THE CHILDREN’S BEST INTERESTS?

I accept that these parties would meet any definition for high conflict. The report writer accepted that the parties need to have parallel not co-operative parenting arrangements. I accept co-operative parenting is not an option. Orders that as far as possible diminish conflict are the best option for these children.

The parents each seek majority time in their care, with overnights around alternate weekends with the other parent.

The ICL put to the report writer the benefits to the children of an arrangement described as “5-5-2-2”. That is, one parent always has time from Monday to Wednesday, and the other Wednesday to Friday. They then alternate weekends. This proposal would retain the same days in each household across the school week. There may be a benefit to Y at his age in not having a long gap between time with his parents. It may assist the children in transporting their belongings between households by maintaining a routine.

The report writer recommended the children live predominantly with one parent. When cross-examined she was concerned that the ICL’s proposal requires a number of changes for the children each week. Changeovers are a time of stress for children due to the potential for exposure to conflict. This is the case regardless of whether the changeover falls on the same day each week, or different days.

I agree with the concerns of the report writer about minimising the number of changeovers each week. Conflict between their parents is a major issue for these children. They are moving between household where the rules are different, consistent with each parent’s different parenting styles. That can be difficult to navigate for children. It is stressful for them. For these reasons I do not propose making orders for the pattern of time promoted by the ICL.

The children have both expressed views of wanting more time with their mother for a considerable period. That desire did not abate over the period they lived with their father. There is nothing in the evidence to suggest that is likely to change. Although not determinative, the strength of those views over time is a matter I take into account.

I am concerned that if the children live predominantly with their father and their desire for more time with their mother remains it will likely cause further conflict and litigation. X started high school this year. As he reaches adolescence he has an ability to act on his views.

The children have been cared for primarily by their mother throughout their lives. Prior to separation little complaint is made by the father about the quality of her care. Certainly, the father did not intervene in her parenting, nor seek to undertake a more significant role.

I propose ordering the children live with their father from Thursday after school to Tuesday before school each alternate week, and otherwise with their mother. This allows the children significant time with their father, but longer periods with their mother. It is not an equal time arrangement, which I am concerned may require a greater degree of cooperation between the parents than they can manage.

Clear arrangements are easier for the parties to follow, and the children to understand. The mother sought orders for time to occur at other times as agreed. The parties in my view do not have the capacity to manage those types of flexible arrangements. It is more likely to lead to conflict between the parents which will involve the children.

Both parties and the ICL seek orders about time during school holidays that would see the children spending extended time with the other parent. I propose ordering in line with the ICL’s Minute that the parties each have one half of the school holidays. That shall occur in two halves, rather than week about as was sought for the first summer holiday. That would entail more changeovers and the potential for problems that can cause. That would be occurring at a time likely proximate to the property hearing, which may increase tension between the parties. Equal time during holidays allows the parents more relaxed time with the children, without the regimen of school and all that entails. There is no issue of risk or otherwise which would necessitate less time for the children with one parent over holiday periods as the mother’s orders suggested.

Both the mother and the father seek orders for time on special occasions. I propose making orders that provide for Country UU Orthodox Easter, Mother’s Day and Father’s Day. The orders for time will vary so that in alternate years the children will be with their parents for Christmas and New Years Eve. ‘Non-Orthodox’ Easter will fall where it falls, noting that is usually in the holidays at the conclusion of Term 1. The difficulties for the children moving between households persuades me to time occurring in alternate years for special days that usually fall within school holidays.

The ICL seeks that changeover occur at school, or otherwise at the entrance to Suburb XX McDonald’s. The father seeks changeover occur at AB Street, or otherwise Suburb XX McDonald’s. I will make the ICL’s order, noting most of the changeovers will take place at school.

The problems demonstrated with telephone and text communication mean there can be no order for either the children or the parents to communicate when they wish. That will likely lead to anxiety for the children to fulfill what they perceive as their parents’ needs.

I propose ordering telephone communication with the parent with whom they do not reside once during the other parents’ time during the school term, once in school holidays, and three times over the long summer holidays. There was a suggestion by counsel for the father for no more than five texts be sent by a parent each week to each child. I propose making that order. Y does not yet have a telephone, so this will only apply to him once the parents agree he should have a device. Text messages used reasonably are an unobtrusive way for children to know that they are in their parent’s thoughts.

WHICH PARTY SHOULD HOLD DECISION-MAKING RESPONSIBILITY?

The parties each sought orders for sole parental responsibility. Each raises concerns that if the other is successful they will be excluded from important matters for the children.

I accept the submissions put by the ICL with respect to parental responsibility that there should be no order for joint parental responsibility. Instead, each parent will retain parental responsibility and decision-making that they hold as a matter of law.

The orders promoted by the ICL provide resolution of matters such as education, general practitioners, and a psychological practice for the children. That limits decisions of a long-term nature the parents may need to determine. I will make those orders. I will make the notation sought that each parent acknowledges they will meet one half of all school fees.

By setting out these matters in orders it is hoped to remove sources of future conflict. I accept that is a sensible approach in this matter.

I do not propose making orders in accordance with order 13 of the ICL’s Minute as there is no issue with respect to that. The parties are of a similar cultural and religious background. That has not been an issue between them and in those circumstances, I decline to make the order.

I am concerned that orders about clothing, and return of clothing, are generally not helpful for parents. Both need clothing for the children, including school uniforms. I appreciate the boys have bags that are cumbersome that they do not like taking to school for time with the other parent. It is for the parents to do what they can to limit items packed by making duplicate items available for use in their home.

Extracurricular activities are not a long-term parenting decision. The parents need to facilitate the boys’ engagement in activities the children wish to attend. If there is no agreement that each parent will take children the boys to activities that fall across both households, the boys do not attend. Orders cannot govern every aspect of the boys’ experience, and the parents need to take responsibility for making arrangements that are workable for the children.

ANCILLARY MATTERS

I propose ordering the parties’ use of the OurFamilyWizard application. This may assist communication. I appreciate it will not improve the parental relationship. It assists by having all information in one place and the parties can use a joint calendar for the boys’ activities. All communication will take place through the application, save for medical emergencies in which case the parties may call each other.

I do not propose making orders about the parties not blocking each other’s mobile telephones. They will be aware there is an order for emergency communication to be by telephone. The parties need to act in accordance with that.

The ICL seeks that the parents advise one another in writing within 48 hours of their residential address, contact mobile telephone number and contact email address, and advise one another of any change to such details in writing within 48 hours of such change. I propose making that order so that each knows where the children are living.

The report writer noted the benefits to both parties of engaging in a counselling or therapeutic relationship to improve their relationship. That is particularly important because property proceedings are on foot. I do not propose making an order to create another battleground. I appreciate there are costs difficulties for the parties in making such an order work.

I accept that it is a matter for the parents if they choose to prioritise engaging in such a process. Unless the parents engage voluntarily it is unlikely they will derive much benefit. For these reasons I will not make that an order.

The parties may have opportunities to travel overseas with the children within the time they spend with the children, or by agreement for longer periods. The mother seeks a Family Law Watchlist order, restraining the father from removing the children from Australia without written consent or a court order. The children will be spending substantial time with their father. There is no evidence of risk that he will remove the children permanently from Australia. I decline to make the mother’s order.

I will make orders in line with the father’s Minute that the father holds X’s passport and the mother holds Y’s passport, which they are required to provide prior to travel overseas.

The restraints sought by the ICL are not opposed by either of the parties and versions of these are sought by all.

I do not propose making orders about use of illicit substances as that is not an issue for these parties on the evidence.

The ICL seeks an order granting leave to the parties to provide a copy of the Orders to any educational, medical or government entities that may seek or require to hold a copy of the orders for the purpose of discharging any duties, legislative, or policy requirements on the condition that the Orders are not further published. I propose making that order as it may assist in limiting future disputes between the parties, leading to further conflict.

The mother seeks orders for provision of information from schools and health care providers to both parties. They both hold parental responsibility, so no order is required.

The mother seeks an order that both parties be at liberty to attend all school functions and events, extra-curricular activities, awards nights, sporting events and other special events for the children. Both parties will be involved in the children’s lives. I will make the order because there is a risk of further argument about attendance at a time the children are in the care of the other parent.

The ICL seeks orders that in the event of a further dispute in relation to the interpretation or effect of the Orders and/or prior to commencement of any future proceedings, the parents participate in Family Dispute Resolution with a family relationship centre or authorised person, and that they share the costs of that equally. The parents have obligations under the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 (Cth) to engage in dispute resolution. I do not propose making that order.

The ICL sought an order for costs to be paid in one half shares. I note the costs are modest and propose making the orders with a significant time to pay given the property proceedings are on foot.

desc.zh: 规定作出养育命令时,子女最佳利益为首要考量。
desc.en: Provides that in making a parenting order the child's best interests are the paramount consideration.

desc.zh: 列出判定子女最佳利益须考量的(非穷尽)事项,含促进安全、子女意见、满足需要的能力等。
desc.en: Sets out the (non-exhaustive) matters to consider in determining a child's best interests, including safety, the child's views, and capacity to meet needs.

desc.zh: 规定父母各自依法享有亲职责任;法院最终不作共同命令,由双方各依此条保有。
desc.en: Provides that each parent has parental responsibility as a matter of law; the court made no joint order, leaving each to retain it under this section.

desc.zh: 第 61D(3) 条容许就重大长期事项分配共同或单独决策权;法院依子女最佳利益判断是否作此命令。
desc.en: Section 61D(3) allows the allocation of joint or sole decision-making on major long-term issues; the court decides by reference to the child's best interests.

summary.zh: 确立「不可接受风险」检验,为各类风险主张的试金石。
summary.en: Lays down the 'unacceptable risk' test, the touchstone for risk claims.

prop.zh: 以不可接受风险为养育安全的核心标准。
prop.en: Unacceptable risk is the central standard for safety in parenting.

summary.zh: 风险评估须考量过往行为以判断风险及其未来程度,是基于证据的结论。
summary.en: Risk assessment considers past conduct to judge risk and its future magnitude, as an evidence-based conclusion.

prop.zh: 风险须基于证据评估,并考量能否减缓。
prop.en: Risk is assessed on the evidence, and whether it can be mitigated.

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

BOYLE J:

These are parenting proceedings in relation to the children of the parties, X who is now 12 years and Y who is now 9 years.
The father seeks that the children live with him and he have sole decision-making responsibility, with the mother to provide her views about decisions to be made for the children. He seeks that the children spend time with the mother each alternate weekend from Thursday afternoon to Monday morning, and half of school holidays.
The mother seeks that the children live with her and she have sole parental responsibility. She seeks that the children spend time with the father each alternate week from Friday afternoon to Sunday evening, and additional days during school holidays.
The Independent Children’s Lawyer (“ICL”) proposed a regime of equal time alternating school weeks and weekends, with both parents to hold parental responsibility and decision-making for the children.
The children currently live with the mother and spend time with the father on alternate weeks from Wednesday to Monday.
The parties have a property dispute which is ongoing before this Court.
For the reasons set out I will order that the children live with their mother, and spend time with their father in alternate weeks from Wednesday to Monday. No order will be made for parental responsibility for the children so that each parent retains parental responsibility under s 61C of the Family law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”). Various orders about health and education have been made as sought by the ICL.

BACKGROUND

The father was born in 1980. The mother was born in 1979.
The parties met and commenced a relationship in 2010. They married in 2012.
The parties separated in late October 2022. Since at least that time there has been a considerable deterioration in the parties’ capacity to communicate.
At separation the mother became aware of a significant tax debt in her name, which she regards as caused by the father. This has been a persistent source of acrimony. They continued living under the same roof until October 2023. Conflict between the parties escalated over this period.
The mother was arrested and charged for drawing a key across the father’s motor vehicle and making threats against him. An AVO was granted for his protection which expired in March 2025. The children lived with the father between November 2023 and December 2024 and spent fortnightly time with the mother.
In December 2024, the mother retained the children in her care. This followed the father being charged with intimidation. He was ultimately acquitted of that charge. An ADVO was granted for the protection of the children. The children were interviewed by police.
The father filed an Application in a Proceeding on 16 January 2025. He had not seen the children since their retention by the mother in December 2024. On 6 February 2025 at a hearing on the father’s recovery order application, I made an order for the children to live with the mother and spend time with the father on alternate weeks from Wednesday to Monday.
At the conclusion of the final hearing the parties had not resolved the issue of school for X for 2026. Orders were made that he be enrolled at a school agreed on by the parents, and failing agreement at F School, so that X could properly prepare for high school.

DOCUMENTS RELIED UPON

The parties relied on the documents set out in their Case Outline Documents.
The parties were cross-examined, as were the wife’s brother Mr H, and Dr K.
A number of documents were tendered during the course of the proceedings. I have had regard to that material, as well as the documents relied upon by the parties.

THE LAW

Part VII of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) deals with the making of parenting orders. Section 60CA of the Act provides that the court must regard the best interests of the child as the paramount consideration in making a parenting order. Section 60CC(2), (2A) and (3) of the Act set out a list of matters to be considered in determining what is in a child’s best interest. It is not an exhaustive list.
In considering what orders would promote the safety of the child, the authorities of M v M (1988) 166 CLR 69; [1988] HCA 68 and Isles & Nelissen (2022) FLC 94-042 are relevant to the assessment of risk. Where there are allegations of risk, such as in this matter, the analysis requires consideration of past conduct to assess whether there is a risk to the child, and the magnitude of that risk in the future. The assessment of risk is an “evidence-based conclusion” (Isles & Nelissen (2022) FLC 94-042 at [85]). The court must then consider whether that risk is capable of being mitigated or ameliorated by imposing restraints or conditions.
I will have regard to relevant matters set out under s 60CC of the Act as they arise in these reasons.
The parties are in dispute about decision-making authority for the children. In determining whether to make an order under s 61D(3) of the Act, the court must consider the best interests of the children and apply the matters set out in s 60CC(2).

ISSUES

The issues in this matter are:
(a) Have either of the parties perpetrated family violence?

(b) What is the capacity of each party to meet the children’s needs?

(c) What live with and spend time with arrangements are in the children’s best interests?

(d) Which party should hold decision-making responsibility?

CREDIT

Both parents had difficulties in acknowledging the impact of their conduct on the children. This was not so much an issue of credit as an issue of perception. This issue is explored below where relevant.

HAVE EITHER OF THE PARTIES PERPETRATED FAMILY VIOLENCE?

There have been a number of allegations relating to family violence made by each party against the other. The complaints need to be viewed in the context of the orders sought by the parties. Each party seek orders that the other spends in alternate weeks three to four consecutive days and nights. The ICL sought orders for equal time in a split arrangement.
The significance of this is that although both parents raised serious issues about the other, there are no concerns of a risk to the children’s safety which would warrant supervision or no overnight time.
The parties lived separately under the one roof from October 2022 for about a year. The separation was initiated after the mother became aware the parties had accumulated a tax debt of about $300,000 in the mother’s name. A sheriff attended the home with respect to the debt. The father does not agree he manipulated the family’s finances to cause that outcome. There can be no issue there is a significant tax debt in the mother’s name. She was unaware of the extent of the liability until October 2022.
Living separately under the one roof is stressful for most separated couples. I accept that it was particularly so for these parties. It has been an ongoing source of grievance for the mother that the father has continued to reside in the home after she vacated the property. The home is the major asset of the parties.
When separated under the one roof the father slept on a blowup mattress separate from the main bedroom. The mother conceded she slashed blowup mattresses used by the father more than once. She did not express any remorse about that.
The boys have been interviewed by police on at least two occasions about allegations of physical violence by their father. The father has been charged but not convicted of any offence.
The mother was charged and convicted of scratching the father’s car with a key during the time of separation under the one roof. She was arrested in front of the children in October 2023. It is clear from her manner giving evidence that she remains angry about this incident, despite acknowledging her conduct. An ADVO was granted for the protection of the father. She regards the father’s conduct with respect to the parties’ financial situation as justifying her conduct.
In October 2023 while the parties were living separately under the one roof, the mother complained to police about the father’s aggressive conduct towards her. While playing basketball with the children he threw a basketball on the wall next to her head multiple times. Her evidence is the children were present and upset. The children have never made any reference to this incident in any interview. The father says he thought the children threw the ball. I do not find on the evidence that incident occurred as described by the mother.
On another occasion the mother alleges the father threw a rubber ball at her which hit the wall next to her. This was at a time of high tension between the parties.
I accept the mother was upset and stressed whilst they were living under the one roof, and extremely sensitive to the father’s behaviour. I accept she felt intimidated by his conduct. I do not accept the mother’s descriptions of the father’s behaviour as deliberate. The available evidence does not support that finding.
I accept when living separated under the one roof the father made videos of the mother. He did so to further his case. He was aware that it incensed her. I accept he controlled when the videos were recorded. Unsurpisingly the videos show her in a poor light swearing and arguing with him. I accept the father’s conduct was provocative.
In October 2023 the mother was arrested for breaching the ADVO. In October 2023 the mother attended Suburb Q police station to make a complaint about the father’s historical conduct towards the children. The complaints involved damage to X’s smartwatch, and that the father threw a ball at him.
The police attended the boys’ school. They spoke to X. He told them that in February 2023 his father was angry and threw a ball, which hit him in the back. He described it as a small ball on a string. He provided no context around the incident. He also referred to being in the car with his father when he became angry and grabbed the “closest thing to him and threw it at the window” which chipped. It was a wearable fitness tracker. Y was also present.
The police determined not to take any action on the complaint. The mother believes that the father’s brother, who is a police officer in Queensland, was able to exert influence over the local police to achieve this outcome. This is a serious allegation made with no evidence in support. I reject that allegation. The police appear to have acted reasonably in light of the complaint, the dearth of evidence, and the context of the parties’ separation.
In December 2024 the mother attended Suburb YY Police Station and made a number of complaints about the father’s conduct towards the children, as reported to her by the boys. The children were interviewed at 8 pm that night. In December 2024 Y was threatened his father would “rip/snap your pinkie toe off”. There are two different versions of the threat. He also threatened to hit Y with a belt. Y told police he was scared of his father when he became angry, which happened when he did not clean up rubbish. There were no injuries to Y. This complaint was first made by Y to his grandmother. She has provided her version of this event.
Both boys had made general complaints to the mother of being hit by their father on their feet, under their arms and on their legs three or four times each week. There were no injuries observed on either child.
X became upset at a sporting competition in December 2024. A friend of the mother approached X. Her son is a friend of X’s. He told her he was upset about how he did in the competition. He asked her if he could speak to his mother. She dialled the mother and gave him her phone.
The father came over to get X. The mother’s friend told him it was okay to speak to his mother. He did that. There is no suggestion the father behaved aggressively towards the child. I accept that X wanted to speak to his mother when he felt upset with his performance. I note that ordinarily the mother would have been present at the sporting competition but was not because of the AVO.
The police recorded when the boys made complaints they used words like “intimidate” and “I’m scared” without “much confidence or explanation”. They mainly complained of feeling scared when their father became angry.
In December 2024 the father was arrested and charged. This happened when the boys were due to be returned to him. They were in the car nearby when he was arrested, as is referred to below. He was ultimately not convicted of any offence. An AVO was granted for the protection of the children.
Y first made a complaint to his grandmother in January 2025, that his father had hit him on the soles of his feet. The mother reported it to the police. X said he was in the room. Y said he was not. The police were concerned their answers to questions “seemed very rehearsed and coached”. They determined not to take any action as the conduct was lawful chastisement and had been talked up by the mother. The boys also alleged their father chased and hit them.
The report writer noted the children were “quick to describe the smacking incident, and Y presented it out of context and devoid of emotion”. She found this unusual. His conduct suggests that he was encouraged to provide the information.
I do not accept that Y was physically abused by his father in January 2025. Photographs were tendered of bruising on the child’s torso. I do not accept that the father has deliberately injured the children. I accept both boys have seen their father angry and been frightened by that experience. That does not amount to family violence that would put the safety of either child at risk.
The mother makes allegations about the father’s conduct towards her and the children during the marriage. She has alleged the father physically abused the children multiple times each week. I do not accept this occurred. It is inconsistent with their presentation and information provided by the children to the report writer. They told the report writer smacking happened once. The children demonstrated no fear towards their father, and they were observed to share a warm relationship. They have maintained across both interviews that they want time with him, albeit for less time than with their mother.
The allegations made by the boys appear to have accelerated during 2024. I accept they were stressed that they were no longer living in their mother’s primary care. They were consistent in their views to a variety of people they wanted increased time with her. It is in those circumstances that many of the allegations were made.
I accept that there may have been times the boys were frightened if the father became angry with them. I do not accept that they have been subjected to family violence by their father or mother. That is in keeping with the orders sought by all three parties that these children require significant time with each of their parents. None of the orders proposed in the matter I accept would place the children at risk of family violence.
The real issue for these children is as identified by Dr K at paragraph 17 of the Update Report dated 5 June 2025:
The parents remain engaged in a prolonged, emotionally charged and high conflict dynamic that has persisted over a significant period and has not eased since the last family assessment. Observations and information gathered during the current assessment suggest that there is a profound lack of trust and mutual regard between them. Their interactions show little evidence of effective communication or co-parenting, and the children have not been adequately protected from exposure to this.

WHAT IS THE CAPACITY OF EACH PARTY TO MEET THE CHILDREN’S NEEDS?

The children are noted by the report writer as “developing appropriately, are without observed mental health issues and do not have any reported special needs”. They need their parents to provide stability and emotional security for them. The report writer referred to them requiring “a safe and conflict free environment”. Both parents have found it difficult to set aside their conflict for the benefit of the children.
This is despite having the views of X from Dr K’s Update Report, that he wants his parents to stop fighting, their fighting is hurting him and if they stopped fighting it would be easier for him. These views were consistent with his views in the first report: “He loves both parents and wants the fighting to stop”. Their fighting makes it difficult for him to move between his parents’ homes as he would like.
I hold concerns that if these heartfelt expressions by their son have not caused a change in the parents’ behaviour there can be little hope that a judgment will affect their conduct.
The parents have different parenting approaches. The mother engages the children in school and extracurriculars in a more regimented manner than the father, who has a more relaxed view. Having different parental styles can benefit children by showing them there can be more than one approach to life, with advantages and disadvantages. Difficulty arises when parents criticise the other’s approach, and appropriate but different parenting styles become a source of conflict which needs to be navigated by the children.
Maternal and paternal family members and friends on affidavit provide support for the parenting capacity of the mother and the father respectively. The affidavits demonstrate the extent to which the parental dispute has involved the wider family and community. Given the arrangements sought by each party there can be real no issue of risk to the children in the care of either parent.
The mother gave X a smartwatch and telephone. It was provided to X without the parents having any common approach to use of devices. The mother outlined in evidence the rules in her home for use of the mobile phone. Those included that the phone remain in the kitchen or dining room overnight, and not in his bedroom. He was able to have the phone in the morning. The school required mobile phones be handed to the office in the mornings until 3 pm. When she gets home between 4 pm and 6 pm, X gives her his phone. He is able to use it after homework is completed. If X overuses the mobile phone, she confiscates it.
The mother has never communicated these rules to the father. He has found managing X’s use of his phone difficult. He has observed X anxiously watching his device for texts.
The mother’s manner of contacting X in his father’s care is completely inconsistent with the rules she imposes in her home. This did not appear to have occurred to her.
The messages between the mother and X on 14 April 2025 demonstrate escalating anxiety in the mother, unrelated to the circumstances for the children. She was unable to contain her anxiety, which X was exposed to.
On that occasion the boys were having a sleepover at their paternal uncle and aunt’s home without their father. There is no suggestion that situation caused any risk for either child. X complained to his mother that he had to sleep on a blowup mattress in the lounge room. The mother sent a number of messages to X similar to this: “your father needs to notify [me] of that... I haven’t agreed to that”. The mother makes clear to X her view that he should be at home sleeping in his own bed; that she did not approve of him sleeping on a blowup mattress; that he should tell his father to come and get him. She offered to collect him.
The mother asked X if he was scared to text his father. She asked him to call her away from the paternal family. When X did not respond quickly enough, she texted he should not ignore her or she would confiscate the phone permanently: “I am your mother and I expect a response”. I accept her responses were driven by anxiety.
Texting of this type continued over a period of five hours. It was extraordinarily intrusive and upsetting for X. It placed an emotional and psychological burden on him for no reason. The mother’s texts make clear she was requiring him to act on her directions, regardless of what he or those caring for him might want. That meant X was required to attend to his mother’s needs, rather than be in the moment with his cousins, brother and other extended paternal family.
Similarly, text messages relating to the children not attending swimming lessons were inappropriate. The mother sent messages such as, “you need to go because I pay for this”. She told X she borrowed money from his grandfather to meet the costs. The mother made X responsible for his attendance at swimming, which he had no control over.
In other text exchanges the mother makes X feel responsible for his brother. An example is telling X he should go to bed early to be with Y, because Y needs his love, and he makes Y feel safe and calm.
On 18 May 2025, the night before interviews for the Update Report, the mother sent a series of texts to X. She gave evidence she was telling X to tell the truth. That is not a fair assessment of the messages sent. She listed matters he should raise with the report writer. When cross-examined she was furious at the father getting a dog two days before the interview. She thought this was sufficient justification for her directing X in the manner she did.
I accept the dog purchase two days before the interview was likely done by the father to promote his position to the boys. Of itself it did not cause harm to the children. This does not justify the mother’s conduct.
An order was made on 6 February 2025 restraining each of the parties from involving the children in the court proceedings and denigrating the other parent. I accept that the mother was not complying with that order.
The text messages with X suggest the mother can react in a compulsive way at times when messaging X. She continues to text after he has responded, on the same topic. The messages place expectations on him which are completely unreasonable. I accept receiving messages from his mother in these circumstances would have been overwhelming and preoccupying for X. The mother’s conduct put X in the middle of conflict between his parents, which she created. She has done this with no thought about the emotional impact on him.
These children have seen both their parents be arrested by the police. In neither case did the other parent attempt to buffer them from that experience.
When their mother was arrested in 2023 her brother was present. He described the children as very upset, yelling, crying and begging for her not to be taken. She was handcuffed and placed in a paddy wagon. Four officers were present. I accept this was a terrible experience for the children. I accept it was an overreaction to her conduct at that time.
When their father was arrested in December 2023 the children were in the car with their maternal uncle and mother. They were driving to a changeover. I accept the mother and her brother expected changeover would not happen, because the father would be arrested.
The mother telephoned the police on hands free in the car. I accept the boys could hear that conversation. The mother stopped near the location for changeover, waiting for the police to arrive. Their uncle left them in the car to observe and record the incident on his telephone.
The uncle’s evidence was contradictory. At one point he said the children “had no awareness of the situation”. I do not accept at any point that could have been true. They heard the telephone call between their mother and the police. Their mother and uncle discussed the situation during the drive.
I accept the mother and her brother exposed the children to their father’s arrest. I accept this would have been at least bewildering if not frightening for the children. Those people were acutely aware of the toll on the children of the mother’s arrest. They had no insight that they were deliberately exposing the children to a repeat of that situation.
It is concerning that the extended families have involved themselves in the dispute. In December 2024 the maternal uncle and maternal grandmother attended a Christmas carol event at the boys’ school. The mother was prevented from attending due to the AVO. The uncle followed the father and children after the event because his car was parked in the same street. He did not wait to let them go, or cross the street. He walked past the father and children, who were getting into their car. The father complained to the police that he was threatened. Footage was available from one of the houses nearby which demonstrated there was no altercation. The police did not take the matter further.
The significance of this occasion is that the boys were again in the middle of conflict between people they love. The mother’s brother had the opportunity to wait and not put the children in that situation.
The mother’s brother has sent text messages to the father such as, “keep up the bullshit charade for someone who doesn’t know you”. He denied the messages were threatening. His evidence is they were “straight to the point”. I accept the texts were unnecessary and could be broadly described as threatening.
The report writer noted that X had an increased level of distress during the interviews for the second report. Y is reported by the mother to have heightened anxiety and panic attacks. The report writer did not allocate responsibility to either parent for the children’s distress, but was concerned at the children’s exposure to their conflict.
The children have spent more time with their mother over the course of their lives. She is the parent connected to the school, their friends’ parents, who makes arrangements for them. Ms ZZ’s affidavit supports the mother’s evidence that during the relationship the father left organising the children to the mother. That is, I accept, the lived experience of these children. It is not a criticism of the father but an observation about how these children have experienced being cared for by their parents. This may well give a greater level of comfort to the children in living in their mother’s home, than their father’s.
The mother’s psychologist has referred to her as having a severe level of anxiety, and depressed mood. The psychologist recommended she have ongoing treatment with a practitioner with experience in family law matters. His last session with the mother was 19 August 2024. There is no evidence she has consulted a psychologist since. The report writer posited six appointments with the psychiatrist Dr AA over almost two years does not suggest therapeutic work, but check ins for medication.
The mother’s anxious behaviour, such as texting X is less likely to be contained by orders that the children spend more time with the father. The evidence does not support that she has engaged in this kind of conduct when the children are with her. Her care for the children prior to separation does not suggest similar difficulties. Rather, the financial issues discovered at separation, the children living primarily with the father after October 2023, and the father remaining in the former matrimonial home all appear to have caused her difficulties in managing her behaviour and emotional response.
I accept that proceedings being finalised will assist the mother’s behaviour. Unfortunately, the property proceedings remain outstanding. The mother should engage with a therapist to manage her anxiety, particularly while litigation remains on foot.

VIEWS

The children are not of an age or stage of maturity when I would consider their views to be determinative, although they should be taken into account.
The children have expressed a desire to live primarily with their mother across the interviews for both reports. The children have presented as close to both parents, although they desire more time with their mother.
I accept that the children missed their mother when they were no longer living with her. This may be a function of her being their primary carer throughout their lives. Despite the forceful submissions made on the father’s part about the mother’s conduct, I note the orders he seeks are for substantial time with her. I accept this is an acknowledgment of the close relationship she and the children share.
At the second interviews X expressed suspicion of his father, such as getting a dog as a bribe. I accept the mother expressed this in texts to X prior to the interview. This was not a conclusion X reached. The report writer was concerned the children’s views at the second report were less candid than for the first report. I take this as indicative of the toll of the conflict on the children.

WHAT LIVE WITH AND SPEND TIME WITH ARRANGEMENTS ARE IN THE CHILDREN’S BEST INTERESTS?

I accept that these parties would meet any definition for high conflict. The report writer accepted that the parties need to have parallel not co-operative parenting arrangements. I accept co-operative parenting is not an option. Orders that as far as possible diminish conflict are the best option for these children.
The parents each seek majority time in their care, with overnights around alternate weekends with the other parent.
The ICL put to the report writer the benefits to the children of an arrangement described as “5-5-2-2”. That is, one parent always has time from Monday to Wednesday, and the other Wednesday to Friday. They then alternate weekends. This proposal would retain the same days in each household across the school week. There may be a benefit to Y at his age in not having a long gap between time with his parents. It may assist the children in transporting their belongings between households by maintaining a routine.
The report writer recommended the children live predominantly with one parent. When cross-examined she was concerned that the ICL’s proposal requires a number of changes for the children each week. Changeovers are a time of stress for children due to the potential for exposure to conflict. This is the case regardless of whether the changeover falls on the same day each week, or different days.
I agree with the concerns of the report writer about minimising the number of changeovers each week. Conflict between their parents is a major issue for these children. They are moving between household where the rules are different, consistent with each parent’s different parenting styles. That can be difficult to navigate for children. It is stressful for them. For these reasons I do not propose making orders for the pattern of time promoted by the ICL.
The children have both expressed views of wanting more time with their mother for a considerable period. That desire did not abate over the period they lived with their father. There is nothing in the evidence to suggest that is likely to change. Although not determinative, the strength of those views over time is a matter I take into account.
I am concerned that if the children live predominantly with their father and their desire for more time with their mother remains it will likely cause further conflict and litigation. X started high school this year. As he reaches adolescence he has an ability to act on his views.
The children have been cared for primarily by their mother throughout their lives. Prior to separation little complaint is made by the father about the quality of her care. Certainly, the father did not intervene in her parenting, nor seek to undertake a more significant role.
I propose ordering the children live with their father from Thursday after school to Tuesday before school each alternate week, and otherwise with their mother. This allows the children significant time with their father, but longer periods with their mother. It is not an equal time arrangement, which I am concerned may require a greater degree of cooperation between the parents than they can manage.
Clear arrangements are easier for the parties to follow, and the children to understand. The mother sought orders for time to occur at other times as agreed. The parties in my view do not have the capacity to manage those types of flexible arrangements. It is more likely to lead to conflict between the parents which will involve the children.
Both parties and the ICL seek orders about time during school holidays that would see the children spending extended time with the other parent. I propose ordering in line with the ICL’s Minute that the parties each have one half of the school holidays. That shall occur in two halves, rather than week about as was sought for the first summer holiday. That would entail more changeovers and the potential for problems that can cause. That would be occurring at a time likely proximate to the property hearing, which may increase tension between the parties. Equal time during holidays allows the parents more relaxed time with the children, without the regimen of school and all that entails. There is no issue of risk or otherwise which would necessitate less time for the children with one parent over holiday periods as the mother’s orders suggested.
Both the mother and the father seek orders for time on special occasions. I propose making orders that provide for Country UU Orthodox Easter, Mother’s Day and Father’s Day. The orders for time will vary so that in alternate years the children will be with their parents for Christmas and New Years Eve. ‘Non-Orthodox’ Easter will fall where it falls, noting that is usually in the holidays at the conclusion of Term 1. The difficulties for the children moving between households persuades me to time occurring in alternate years for special days that usually fall within school holidays.
The ICL seeks that changeover occur at school, or otherwise at the entrance to Suburb XX McDonald’s. The father seeks changeover occur at AB Street, or otherwise Suburb XX McDonald’s. I will make the ICL’s order, noting most of the changeovers will take place at school.
The problems demonstrated with telephone and text communication mean there can be no order for either the children or the parents to communicate when they wish. That will likely lead to anxiety for the children to fulfill what they perceive as their parents’ needs.
I propose ordering telephone communication with the parent with whom they do not reside once during the other parents’ time during the school term, once in school holidays, and three times over the long summer holidays. There was a suggestion by counsel for the father for no more than five texts be sent by a parent each week to each child. I propose making that order. Y does not yet have a telephone, so this will only apply to him once the parents agree he should have a device. Text messages used reasonably are an unobtrusive way for children to know that they are in their parent’s thoughts.

WHICH PARTY SHOULD HOLD DECISION-MAKING RESPONSIBILITY?

The parties each sought orders for sole parental responsibility. Each raises concerns that if the other is successful they will be excluded from important matters for the children.
I accept the submissions put by the ICL with respect to parental responsibility that there should be no order for joint parental responsibility. Instead, each parent will retain parental responsibility and decision-making that they hold as a matter of law.
The orders promoted by the ICL provide resolution of matters such as education, general practitioners, and a psychological practice for the children. That limits decisions of a long-term nature the parents may need to determine. I will make those orders. I will make the notation sought that each parent acknowledges they will meet one half of all school fees.
By setting out these matters in orders it is hoped to remove sources of future conflict. I accept that is a sensible approach in this matter.
I do not propose making orders in accordance with order 13 of the ICL’s Minute as there is no issue with respect to that. The parties are of a similar cultural and religious background. That has not been an issue between them and in those circumstances, I decline to make the order.
I am concerned that orders about clothing, and return of clothing, are generally not helpful for parents. Both need clothing for the children, including school uniforms. I appreciate the boys have bags that are cumbersome that they do not like taking to school for time with the other parent. It is for the parents to do what they can to limit items packed by making duplicate items available for use in their home.
Extracurricular activities are not a long-term parenting decision. The parents need to facilitate the boys’ engagement in activities the children wish to attend. If there is no agreement that each parent will take children the boys to activities that fall across both households, the boys do not attend. Orders cannot govern every aspect of the boys’ experience, and the parents need to take responsibility for making arrangements that are workable for the children.

ANCILLARY MATTERS

I propose ordering the parties’ use of the OurFamilyWizard application. This may assist communication. I appreciate it will not improve the parental relationship. It assists by having all information in one place and the parties can use a joint calendar for the boys’ activities. All communication will take place through the application, save for medical emergencies in which case the parties may call each other.
I do not propose making orders about the parties not blocking each other’s mobile telephones. They will be aware there is an order for emergency communication to be by telephone. The parties need to act in accordance with that.
The ICL seeks that the parents advise one another in writing within 48 hours of their residential address, contact mobile telephone number and contact email address, and advise one another of any change to such details in writing within 48 hours of such change. I propose making that order so that each knows where the children are living.
The report writer noted the benefits to both parties of engaging in a counselling or therapeutic relationship to improve their relationship. That is particularly important because property proceedings are on foot. I do not propose making an order to create another battleground. I appreciate there are costs difficulties for the parties in making such an order work.
I accept that it is a matter for the parents if they choose to prioritise engaging in such a process. Unless the parents engage voluntarily it is unlikely they will derive much benefit. For these reasons I will not make that an order.
The parties may have opportunities to travel overseas with the children within the time they spend with the children, or by agreement for longer periods. The mother seeks a Family Law Watchlist order, restraining the father from removing the children from Australia without written consent or a court order. The children will be spending substantial time with their father. There is no evidence of risk that he will remove the children permanently from Australia. I decline to make the mother’s order.
I will make orders in line with the father’s Minute that the father holds X’s passport and the mother holds Y’s passport, which they are required to provide prior to travel overseas.
The restraints sought by the ICL are not opposed by either of the parties and versions of these are sought by all.
I do not propose making orders about use of illicit substances as that is not an issue for these parties on the evidence.
The ICL seeks an order granting leave to the parties to provide a copy of the Orders to any educational, medical or government entities that may seek or require to hold a copy of the orders for the purpose of discharging any duties, legislative, or policy requirements on the condition that the Orders are not further published. I propose making that order as it may assist in limiting future disputes between the parties, leading to further conflict.
The mother seeks orders for provision of information from schools and health care providers to both parties. They both hold parental responsibility, so no order is required.
The mother seeks an order that both parties be at liberty to attend all school functions and events, extra-curricular activities, awards nights, sporting events and other special events for the children. Both parties will be involved in the children’s lives. I will make the order because there is a risk of further argument about attendance at a time the children are in the care of the other parent.
The ICL seeks orders that in the event of a further dispute in relation to the interpretation or effect of the Orders and/or prior to commencement of any future proceedings, the parents participate in Family Dispute Resolution with a family relationship centre or authorised person, and that they share the costs of that equally. The parents have obligations under the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 (Cth) to engage in dispute resolution. I do not propose making that order.
The ICL sought an order for costs to be paid in one half shares. I note the costs are modest and propose making the orders with a significant time to pay given the property proceedings are on foot.
